questions.question: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
questions.quickAnswer: DELETE removes rows one at a time with WHERE clause support and is logged; TRUNCATE removes all rows instantly and resets identity columns but cannot be filtered; DROP removes the entire table structure and data permanently.
keyPoints: DELETE: DML, rows removed one by one, WHERE clause allowed, can be rolled back in a transaction, triggers fire, does NOT reset AUTO_INCREMENT
TRUNCATE: DDL, removes all rows in a single operation, no WHERE clause, resets AUTO_INCREMENT, much faster, triggers do NOT fire, implicit commit in MySQL
DROP: DDL, removes table definition + all data + indexes + constraints permanently, cannot be rolled back in MySQL
In MySQL, TRUNCATE causes an implicit commit — it cannot be rolled back even inside a transaction
DELETE is the only option when you need to selectively remove rows or maintain audit logs via triggers

questions.question: What is the difference between a stored procedure, function, and view in MySQL?
questions.quickAnswer: A stored procedure is a named block of SQL that can perform DML/DDL and returns zero or more result sets; a function returns exactly one value and can be used inside SELECT; a view is a saved SELECT query that acts like a virtual table.
keyPoints: Stored procedure: called with CALL, can have IN/OUT/INOUT params, can include transactions and DML, cannot be used inside SELECT
Function: called inside expressions (SELECT, WHERE), must return a scalar value, cannot perform DML in MySQL without special config
View: a saved SELECT query, no stored logic, can be queried like a table, updatable views have restrictions
Materialized view: MySQL does not natively support these — simulated with a physical table + scheduled refresh job
Use procedures for complex business logic; use functions for reusable computed values; use views for simplifying complex joins

questions.question: How does InnoDB implement MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)?
questions.quickAnswer: InnoDB maintains multiple versions of each row using hidden system columns (trx_id, roll_pointer) and undo logs. A read transaction sees the snapshot of data as it was at the start of its transaction, without blocking writers.
keyPoints: Every row has two hidden columns: DB_TRX_ID (last transaction that modified the row) and DB_ROLL_PTR (pointer to undo log entry)
When a row is updated, the old version is written to the undo log; the new version is written in place with the new trx_id
A SELECT reads a 'read view' — it ignores rows whose trx_id is newer than the view's snapshot
REPEATABLE READ (default): read view is created at first read in transaction → consistent snapshot throughout
READ COMMITTED: read view is created at each statement → sees committed changes from other transactions
Undo log is purged when no active transaction needs the old version (purge thread)

questions.question: What are phantom reads and how does InnoDB prevent them?
questions.quickAnswer: A phantom read occurs when a transaction re-executes a range query and sees new rows inserted by another committed transaction. InnoDB prevents phantoms in REPEATABLE READ using next-key locks (gap locks + record locks).
keyPoints: Phantom read: T1 reads rows WHERE salary > 50000 (gets 3 rows), T2 inserts a new row with salary 60000 and commits, T1 re-reads and gets 4 rows
InnoDB REPEATABLE READ prevents phantoms for consistent reads (snapshots via MVCC)
For locking reads (SELECT FOR UPDATE / SELECT LOCK IN SHARE MODE), InnoDB uses next-key locks to lock the gap between existing rows
Gap lock: locks the gap between two index values to prevent inserts in that range
Next-key lock = record lock + gap lock on the preceding gap
READ COMMITTED does NOT prevent phantom reads — each statement gets a fresh snapshot

questions.question: How does MySQL detect and resolve deadlocks?
questions.quickAnswer: InnoDB continuously detects deadlocks using a wait-for graph. When a cycle is detected, it automatically rolls back the transaction with the smallest undo log (least work done) and returns error 1213 to the application.
keyPoints: InnoDB maintains a wait-for graph of which transactions are waiting for which locks
Deadlock detected when a cycle exists in the graph (T1 waits for T2, T2 waits for T1)
InnoDB rolls back the 'victim' — typically the transaction with the least undo data (fewest rows modified)
Application must handle error 1213 (ER_LOCK_DEADLOCK) by retrying the transaction
Prevention strategies: always acquire locks in the same order, keep transactions short, use SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock rows early
SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS shows the last deadlock details
questions.codeExample:
-- Deadlock-prone pattern (acquire locks in different order)
-- T1: UPDATE accounts SET bal=bal-100 WHERE id=1; then UPDATE ... WHERE id=2
-- T2: UPDATE accounts SET bal=bal-100 WHERE id=2; then UPDATE ... WHERE id=1

-- Prevention: enforce consistent lock order
-- Always UPDATE WHERE id = MIN(id1,id2) first, then MAX(id1,id2)

-- Check last deadlock
SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS\G

questions.question: What is the difference between clustered and non-clustered indexes in MySQL?
questions.quickAnswer: A clustered index determines the physical order of rows on disk — InnoDB always clusters data by the primary key. A non-clustered (secondary) index stores a copy of the indexed columns plus the primary key, requiring a second lookup to fetch the full row.
keyPoints: InnoDB: every table has exactly one clustered index (the PRIMARY KEY). Rows are stored in B+ tree leaf nodes ordered by PK
If no PK is defined, InnoDB uses the first UNIQUE NOT NULL column; otherwise creates a hidden 6-byte rowid
Secondary index leaf nodes store: indexed columns + primary key value (NOT row pointer) — allows PK changes without rebuilding all secondary indexes
Lookup via secondary index: B+ tree search on secondary index → get PK → B+ tree search on clustered index (this double lookup is called 'row lookup' or 'bookmark lookup')
Covering index avoids the row lookup — if all needed columns are in the index
Choose PK carefully: UUID PKs cause fragmentation; sequential integer PKs give optimal insert performance
questions.codeExample:
-- PK = clustered index
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,      -- clustered
  user_id INT,
  status VARCHAR(20),
  INDEX idx_user (user_id) -- secondary (non-clustered)
);

-- Secondary index lookup for: SELECT id, user_id FROM orders WHERE user_id=5
-- 1. Search idx_user B+tree for user_id=5 → get id values
-- 2. For each id, fetch full row from clustered index (row lookup)

-- Covering index: no row lookup needed
INDEX idx_user_id_covering (user_id, id) -- covers SELECT id WHERE user_id=5

questions.question: What is a covering index and when should you use it?
questions.quickAnswer: A covering index includes all columns needed to satisfy a query — MySQL can answer it entirely from the index without accessing the table rows. It eliminates the row-lookup step and dramatically reduces I/O for read-heavy queries.
keyPoints: An index 'covers' a query when all SELECTed columns + WHERE/ORDER BY columns exist in the index
EXPLAIN shows 'Using index' in the Extra column when a covering index is used
No heap/clustered index access needed → fewer disk reads, better cache utilization
Composite index column order matters: (a, b, c) covers WHERE a=1 AND b=2, but NOT WHERE b=2 alone
Trade-off: wider indexes consume more disk space and slow down INSERT/UPDATE
Common pattern: add frequently SELECTed columns as trailing columns in an existing WHERE-column index

questions.question: How do you read and interpret an EXPLAIN plan in MySQL?
questions.quickAnswer: EXPLAIN shows how MySQL executes a query: the access method (ALL=full scan, ref=index lookup), which index is used, estimated rows scanned, and extra details like Using filesort or Using temporary. Optimize for low rows and good access type.
keyPoints: type column (best to worst): system → const → eq_ref → ref → range → index → ALL
ALL = full table scan — usually bad for large tables
key column: which index MySQL chose; key_len: how much of the index is used
rows column: estimated rows examined (not returned) — lower is better
Extra column warnings: 'Using filesort' = needs sort pass, 'Using temporary' = temp table created, 'Using index' = covering index (good)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0+) shows actual row counts and timing, not just estimates
questions.codeExample:
EXPLAIN SELECT o.id, u.name
FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.status = 'PENDING'
ORDER BY o.created_at DESC\G

-- Look for:
-- type: ref or range (good) vs ALL (bad)
-- key: index name being used
-- rows: 10 (good) vs 100000 (bad)
-- Extra: 'Using filesort' → add index on (status, created_at)

-- Fix: composite index
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date ON orders (status, created_at DESC);

questions.question: What is table partitioning in MySQL and when should you use it?
questions.quickAnswer: Partitioning divides a table's rows into multiple physical partitions based on a partition key. MySQL routes queries to only relevant partitions (partition pruning), reducing I/O for large tables. Use for time-series data where old partitions can be archived or dropped.
keyPoints: Types: RANGE (by value range), LIST (by discrete values), HASH (even distribution), KEY (similar to HASH but uses MySQL's hashing)
Partition pruning: MySQL reads only partitions that match the WHERE clause — avoids full table scan
Dropping a partition is instant (O(1)) vs DELETE which is O(rows) — huge benefit for log/event tables
Every unique/primary key must include the partition column
Not a replacement for indexing — still need indexes within partitions
RANGE COLUMNS allows multiple columns and non-integer types
questions.codeExample:
CREATE TABLE events (
  id BIGINT,
  created_at DATE,
  event_type VARCHAR(50),
  PRIMARY KEY (id, created_at)
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(created_at)) (
  PARTITION p2022 VALUES LESS THAN (2023),
  PARTITION p2023 VALUES LESS THAN (2024),
  PARTITION p2024 VALUES LESS THAN (2025),
  PARTITION pmax  VALUES LESS THAN MAXVALUE
);

-- Drop old data instantly:
ALTER TABLE events DROP PARTITION p2022;

-- Pruning in action:
SELECT * FROM events WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';
-- Only reads p2024 partition

questions.question: What is the difference between sharding and partitioning?
questions.quickAnswer: Partitioning splits a table's data across multiple physical locations within a single database server. Sharding splits data across multiple independent database servers (nodes), each owning a subset of the data — it's horizontal scaling at the infrastructure level.
keyPoints: Partitioning: one server, one database, one logical table — MySQL manages routing internally
Sharding: multiple servers (shards), each server has its own database instance — application/proxy manages routing
Shard key determines which shard a row belongs to (e.g., user_id % 4)
Sharding challenges: cross-shard joins are expensive or impossible, distributed transactions, rebalancing data when adding shards
Consistent hashing minimizes rebalancing when adding/removing shards
Most applications should exhaust vertical scaling + read replicas + partitioning before considering sharding

questions.question: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
questions.quickAnswer: UNION combines results from two queries and removes duplicate rows using an implicit DISTINCT operation. UNION ALL combines results and keeps all rows including duplicates — it is faster because it skips the deduplication step.
keyPoints: UNION: internally sorts or hashes both result sets to identify duplicates — O(n log n) cost
UNION ALL: simply concatenates the two result sets — O(n) cost, always faster
Both require the same number of columns with compatible data types in each SELECT
Use UNION ALL when you know results are distinct (e.g., different tables, disjoint WHERE clauses) or when duplicates are acceptable
Window functions like RANK() work on the final combined result set after UNION
ORDER BY and LIMIT apply to the entire result — put them at the end, not inside individual SELECTs
questions.codeExample:
-- UNION (deduplicates)
SELECT email FROM customers
UNION
SELECT email FROM newsletter_subscribers;
-- If same email appears in both tables, appears once

-- UNION ALL (keeps all rows)
SELECT 'sale' AS type, amount FROM sales_2023
UNION ALL
SELECT 'sale' AS type, amount FROM sales_2024;
-- No dedup needed — different year tables, no overlap

-- Performance: UNION ALL is always preferred when duplicates are OK

questions.question: What is the difference between a correlated and non-correlated subquery?
questions.quickAnswer: A non-correlated subquery executes once and its result is used by the outer query. A correlated subquery references columns from the outer query and re-executes for each row processed by the outer query — making it potentially very slow.
keyPoints: Non-correlated: inner query has no reference to outer query; MySQL executes it once and caches the result
Correlated: inner query references outer.column; re-runs for every row in the outer query — O(n×m) complexity
Correlated subqueries are often rewritable as JOINs or window functions, which are faster
EXISTS with a correlated subquery short-circuits on first match — more efficient than IN for large result sets
IN with a non-correlated subquery: MySQL may convert to a semi-join internally (optimizer)
Use EXPLAIN to verify whether MySQL materializes or re-executes the subquery
questions.codeExample:
-- Non-correlated (runs once)
SELECT name FROM employees
WHERE dept_id IN (SELECT id FROM departments WHERE budget > 1000000);

-- Correlated (runs once per employee row — slow)
SELECT name, salary FROM employees e
WHERE salary > (
  SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id -- references e.dept_id
);

-- Rewritten as JOIN (faster)
SELECT e.name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN (SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id) d
  ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > d.avg_sal;

questions.question: How does NULL behave in SQL comparisons and aggregations?
questions.quickAnswer: NULL represents an unknown value. Any comparison with NULL (=, !=, >, <) returns NULL (not TRUE or FALSE), which evaluates as false in WHERE clauses. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to check for NULL. Aggregate functions like SUM/COUNT/AVG ignore NULL values.
keyPoints: NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE — use IS NULL instead
NULL != 'value' also evaluates to NULL — rows with NULL in the column are excluded from WHERE clauses
COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) ignores NULLs in that column
SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX all ignore NULL values — AVG(1, NULL, 3) = 2 (not 1.33)
COALESCE(col, default) returns the first non-NULL value — use to substitute defaults
NULL in GROUP BY: MySQL groups all NULLs together into one group

questions.question: What are window functions in SQL and how do they differ from GROUP BY?
questions.quickAnswer: Window functions compute a value across a set of rows related to the current row (a 'window') without collapsing rows like GROUP BY does. Each row retains its identity while gaining access to aggregated or ranking information.
keyPoints: GROUP BY collapses rows into groups — you lose individual rows
Window functions keep all rows and add a computed column based on a partition/ordering
PARTITION BY: defines the group/window (like GROUP BY but per-row)
ORDER BY inside OVER(): defines the ordering within the window
Aggregate window functions: SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MIN(), MAX() with OVER()
Ranking functions: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK(), NTILE()
Offset functions: LAG(), LEAD(), FIRST_VALUE(), LAST_VALUE()
questions.codeExample:
-- Running total per department
SELECT
  name,
  dept_id,
  salary,
  SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary) AS running_total,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank,
  LAG(salary, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary) AS prev_salary
FROM employees;

-- Top N per group (no GROUP BY needed)
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM employees
) ranked WHERE rnk <= 3;

questions.question: What are CTEs (Common Table Expressions) and when should you use them over subqueries?
questions.quickAnswer: A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with the WITH clause, valid only for the duration of the query. CTEs improve readability for complex queries and enable recursive queries. Unlike subqueries, a CTE can be referenced multiple times in the same query.
keyPoints: Syntax: WITH cte_name AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM cte_name
Multiple CTEs: WITH cte1 AS (...), cte2 AS (...) SELECT ... — cte2 can reference cte1
Recursive CTE: WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (base UNION ALL recursive_step) — used for hierarchical data (org charts, file trees)
MySQL 8.0+ supports CTEs; MySQL 5.x does not
CTEs may or may not be materialized by the optimizer — no guaranteed performance benefit over subqueries
Use CTEs for: multi-step transformations, recursive queries, self-joins, or wherever a meaningful name improves clarity
questions.codeExample:
-- Simple CTE
WITH dept_avg AS (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.avg_sal
FROM employees e
JOIN dept_avg d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > d.avg_sal;

-- Recursive CTE: org hierarchy
WITH RECURSIVE org AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, o.depth + 1
  FROM employees e JOIN org o ON e.manager_id = o.id
)
SELECT * FROM org ORDER BY depth;
